title: Notification preferences can be modified for any wallet without ownership proof
reasoning: The frontend API contract identifies the target account only by a client-supplied wallet address. A connected wallet address in wagmi is not server authentication, and these requests include no nonce, signature, session token, or SIWE-style proof. If the API accepts this contract, any caller can fetch or overwrite notification preferences for another address and potentially link their Telegram chat to another wallet's escrow events.
reproduction: Send POST /api/notifications/preferences with an arbitrary victim wallet address and attacker-controlled telegramChatId/telegramEnabled values; the frontend contract supplies no cryptographic proof the requester controls that wallet.
recommendation: Require wallet ownership proof before reading or writing preferences, for example a signed nonce/SIWE flow bound to the wallet and server session, or include and verify a fresh signature on each preference mutation. Do not rely on a wallet address supplied in the request body or query string as authorization.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: The feature declares no linked tests, and the included component tests do not exercise API authorization semantics.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add an API/integration test that attempts to read or update preferences for a wallet without a valid signature/session and expects 401/403, plus a success case with a verified signature for the same wallet.
minimumFixScope: Notification preference read/write contract and the Telegram wallet-link flow.
